title: LIFT: Language-Interfaced Fine-Tuning for Non-Language Machine Learning Tasks
authors: Tuan Dinh*, Yuchen Zeng* (equal), Ruisu Zhang, Ziqian Lin, Michael Gira, Shashank Rajput, Jy-yong Sohn, Dimitris Papailiopoulos, Kangwook Lee (University of Wisconsin-Madison)
tokenization_method: TEXT SERIALIZATION + the LLM's own subword tokenizer, then standard autoregressive fine-tuning (NOT a learned tabular tokenizer). Each labeled row is rendered into ONE English sentence with a fixed template and fine-tuned with the ordinary next-token cross-entropy loss; NO architecture change, NO new input/output head, NO new loss. Generic template (no feature names): 'When we have x1=<r.x1>, x2=<r.x2>, ..., xp=<r.xp>, what should be y? ### y = <r.y> @@@' where '###' is the OpenAI-recommended question/answer separator and '@@@' is the end-of-answer stop token. Context-aware template (when feature names + a task description exist): each value is placed under its real column name in a natural sentence, e.g. Iris -> 'An Iris plant with sepal length <v>, sepal width <v>, petal length <v>, and petal width <v> is <class>.' Seven prompt variants studied (Sec 4.1). Inference: the test row is serialized with the answer part left blank, the fine-tuned LM completes it, and the output is parsed -- for classification, string-match the completion to a class name (a non-matching string is counted as a misclassification, though nearest-class string metrics could recover it); for regression, split on '@@@' and string->float parse, retrying with decoding temperature raised 0->0.75 up to 5 times, falling back to the train-set mean if all attempts fail (invalid outputs are rare, ~<=1).
feature_types_handled: numerical (continuous synthetic functions, OpenML tabular numerics, MNIST pixels) and categorical (OpenML categorical columns, class labels) -- all flattened verbatim into one text string; the tokenizer does NOT distinguish numeric from categorical. Missing values: not a studied axis (synthetic + clean OpenML/vision data). Temporal: NOT handled at all -- there are no time-series experiments; LIFT is a row-at-a-time i.i.d. supervised setup. Targets handled for BOTH classification (parse class string) and regression (parse float), which distinguishes LIFT from TabLLM (classification-focused).
token_granularity: subword tokens of a natural-language sentence (GPT-J / GPT-3 BPE). One tabular field has NO fixed token correspondence: a number like 10.35 splits into several subwords ('10', '.', '35'), feature names span multiple tokens, and total sequence length grows with the number of features times their string length. This is the opposite of FT-Transformer's one-token-per-feature design; granularity is whatever the text tokenizer emits, hard-capped by the LM context window.
numerical_strategy: RAW numeric value written as a decimal DIGIT STRING inside the sentence; no binning, scaling, normalization, piecewise-linear, periodic, or quantile embedding (the paper notes LIFT 'can directly use raw data while most baselines require feature scaling'). The LM compares/generates tokens, not numeric magnitudes, so regression error is measured over tokens not values -- the authors explicitly conjecture that 'level encoding, i.e., representing numerical values as binary values' could improve regression, conceding raw-digit serialization is the weak point for continuous targets/features.
categorical_strategy: the category's natural-language STRING (or the generic x_i=value form) is written verbatim into the sentence; no lookup embedding table, one-hot, hashing, or target encoding on the LIFT side. Semantics ride on the LM's pretrained word embeddings -- the same column-name-as-semantics idea as TabLLM/CARTE.
high_cardinality_handling: no dedicated mechanism. High-cardinality categoricals would simply spell out more text, consuming the context budget; not studied. Large LABEL cardinality is studied and is a documented failure mode: on the 100-class Margin (64 features) and Texture (64 features) OpenML datasets LIFT/GPT-J drops to ~50% vs RBF-SVM ~82% (a ~30-point gap), i.e. many target classes hurt sharply.
temporal_handling: none. No time-varying features, patching, recurrence, or channel-as-token anywhere in the paper. Provides zero direct evidence for the temporal half of the topic's mixed feature set.
scales_to_many_features: WEAK-to-NEGATIVE for the topic's 70+-feature setting, with mixed surface signals. Headline: 'low-dimensional' tasks (the abstract's own framing). The CLASSIFICATION table does include high-p datasets -- Spambase 57 feat, OPT 64, Margin/Texture 64, Hill-Valley 100 (LIFT/GPT-J 100% vs LogReg 78%), Mfeat 216, MNIST 784 (LIFT/GPT-3 98.15%) -- so on classification LIFT can ingest hundreds of features and still be competitive, BUT the paper repeatedly states 'the limited number of tokens as inputs to LMs restricts the number of features LIFT can input', and the high-feature wins are largely on image/digit data where pixels are simple small integers, NOT 70+ semantically rich heterogeneous mixed-type columns. REGRESSION fails outright in high dimensions: on synthetic Linear p=50, LIFT/GPT-3 RAE 1.18 vs GP 0.13; p=100 even worse; LIFT/GPT-J could NOT be run on 50D/120D at all 'due to memory resource limitation'. So feature-count scaling is bounded by context length, is far more fragile for regression than classification, and was never tested on 70+ mixed numerical+categorical+temporal columns.
downstream_model: the LLM itself is the predictor (no separate head). Two models: GPT-J (6B, open) fine-tuned with LoRA (low-rank parameter-efficient); GPT-3 (175B) fine-tuned through OpenAI's black-box fine-tuning API. Rand-GPT-J variant fine-tunes the WHOLE randomly-initialized model (no LoRA) as an ablation.
pretraining_objective: none added by LIFT -- pure transfer from each LM's existing natural-language pretraining, adapted by ordinary supervised fine-tuning on serialized rows. Ablation (Table 8) isolates the role of pretraining: LIFT/Rand-GPT-J (no pretraining) collapses to ~MCC (e.g. Iris 27.8%, Blobs 25.7%); code-pretrained CodeGen/CodeParrot are far worse than NL-pretrained GPTs; GPT-J fine-tuned on gibberish is only slightly worse than GPT-J -- conclusion: large scale alone is NOT enough, natural-language pretraining is the load-bearing ingredient. Section 5.1 adds an OPTIONAL two-stage 'pretext' warm-up: fine-tune 2-3 epochs on synthetic Gaussian data sharing the target's feature count + label space BEFORE the real data, which lowers sample complexity in the low-data regime (GPT-J only; GPT-3 API can't fix sample order).
key_results: Classification (Table 4, accuracy, OpenML + synthetic + vision): LIFT/GPTs usually rank in the top-3 and match strong baselines. Learns nonlinear boundaries logistic regression cannot (circles: LIFT/GPT-3 81.2% vs LogReg 48.6% ~= MCC 50%; two-circles 81.4% vs LogReg 49.8%). Competitive with XGBoost on tabular (Spambase 94.9 vs XG 95.9; OPT 99.0 vs XG 97.5; Customers 84.9 vs LogReg 87.1). MNIST 98.15% (GPT-3), Fashion-MNIST 90.2%. Degrades hard with many CLASSES (Margin/Texture 100-class: ~50-67% vs SVM ~82%). Regression (Tables 19-20, RAE/RMSE): well approximates 2D functions (linear RAE 0.10) but never beats the strongest baseline (GP) and FAILS in high dimensions (p=50/100). vs ICL (Table 5): LIFT on full data > in-context learning; at equal sample count LIFT/Subset ~= ICL. Robustness: very robust to training-set OUTLIERS (regression) and label corruption, but vulnerable to TEST-time feature corruption / transferred adversarial perturbation (MNIST eps=0.01 drops to 44.9%) unless trained with data augmentation. Calibrated: prediction variance tracks injected noise std. Can also do generative tasks (MNIST image generation/completion via text).
baselines_compared: Classification: logistic regression, decision tree, KNN, RBF-SVM, 4-layer ReLU MLP (200/hidden), random forest, XGBoost, majority-class classifier (MCC). Regression: polynomial regression, kernel ridge (RBF), KNN, 3-layer ReLU MLP (50/hidden), gradient boosting trees, random forest, Gaussian process. Also in-context learning (ICL) with the same LMs. Hyperparameters chosen by grid-search + cross-validation. NOTE: no FT-Transformer / TabTransformer / SAINT / TabPFN deep-tabular baselines -- comparison is vs classical ML + MLP only.
transferable_idea: The reusable nugget for the 70+-feature topic is the same column-name-as-semantics + verbatim-value serialization idea (shared with TabLLM and CARTE) plus the SHUFFLED-NAMES ablation protocol (Table 9): randomly permuting which feature name attaches to which value drops accuracy significantly, proving the model genuinely uses correct feature-name/value association -- a clean test to import when validating any name-aware tokenizer. Secondary transferable techniques: (a) two-stage pretext warm-up on synthetic data matching the target's feature/label structure to cut sample complexity; (b) data augmentation (noisy copies) to harden against test-time feature corruption; (c) the level/binary-encoding-of-numbers conjecture as a pointer to why raw-digit numerics are weak. The verbatim text-serialization MECHANISM itself is NOT directly reusable at 70+ rich heterogeneous features (context-length wall, raw-digit numerics, no temporal story, regression collapse).
limitations: Authors-stated: (1) does NOT perform well when features are high-dimensional (regression) -- empirically fails at p=50/100. (2) does NOT perform well when the number of CLASSES is large (e.g. 100s) -- ~30-pt gap to SVM. (3) context length is bounded by the LM's context window, so feature count is capped. (4) LIFT/GPT is MEMORY-INEFFICIENT (could not even run high-dim regression on GPT-J due to memory); they suggest memory-efficient LMs (Linear Transformer) as a fix. (5) regression error is measured token-wise not value-wise, hurting numeric accuracy (raw-digit weakness). (6) GPT-3 path is a closed black-box API (no control over weights or, for two-stage, sample order). Open questions raised: are LMs ensemble/tree-like; are they universal across modalities; can numeric encoding be improved.
pitfalls: 1) SCOPE MISMATCH (topic-critical): the paper's own framing is 'low-dimensional' tasks; high-feature classification wins are mostly on image/digit pixels (simple small integers), NOT 70+ semantically rich mixed numerical+categorical columns -- do not read MNIST-784 or Mfeat-216 as evidence for the user's setting. 2) REGRESSION COLLAPSE in high-D (Linear p=50 RAE 1.18 vs GP 0.13; p=100 worse; GPT-J unrunnable) -- if the user's target is continuous, LIFT is the wrong anchor. 3) NO temporal handling and NO missing-value handling -- zero coverage of the time-varying/static-fusion half of the topic. 4) NUMERICS are raw digit strings; the authors themselves conjecture level/binary encoding is needed -- do not import as a numerical-tokenization recipe. 5) Deep-tabular baselines (FT-Transformer/SAINT/TabPFN) are ABSENT; 'comparable to baselines' means classical ML + MLP, so it does not establish LIFT vs the SOTA tokenizers this topic actually cares about. 6) Context/memory wall is explicit -- feature count is hard-capped by the LM window, same ceiling as TabLLM. 7) PARTIALLY CONTRADICTS TabLLM on feature-name importance: LIFT's shuffled-names ablation shows a clear drop (names matter), and earlier reports that fine-tuned GPT-3 could not confirm name importance at fractional data are model/setting dependent -- treat 'feature names help' as conditional on the LM having relevant pretraining knowledge of those names (helps most when names are common-knowledge interpretable, per Table 9; CMC sees little gain).
relevance_to_topic: Foundational llm-tabular-serialization anchor (NeurIPS 2022, sibling to TabLLM/GReaT): proves a heterogeneous row can be 'tokenized' purely by text serialization + LM fine-tuning with no learned tabular tokenizer, covering BOTH classification and regression -- but its explicit 'low-dimensional' scope, regression collapse in high-D, context/memory ceiling, raw-digit numerics, and absent temporal/missing handling make it a cautionary historical anchor, not a recipe, for the 70+-mixed-feature target. Reusable parts are the shuffled-names validation protocol, the column-name semantics idea, and the two-stage/augmentation tricks.
code_available: yes -- https://github.com/UW-Madison-Lee-Lab/LanguageInterfacedFineTuning (serialization templates + GPT-J LoRA fine-tuning + GPT-3 API scripts; regression/classification/vision experiments).
